learning_outcomes.description: Differentiate between the different conceptual and theoretical approaches to defining privacy.
Explain the different legal approaches that underpin legal protections of individual privacy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of different legal approaches to privacy.
Apply relevant Australian and international privacy law to contemporary privacy problems.
Reflect on your own understandings of privacy and privacy law.
Have sufficient practical skills to provide advice to potential clients with privacy issues.

assessments.due_date:
3/08/2026 - 26/10/2026

Mini quiz will take place in class each week, other than in Week 1.
assessments.task_description:
There will be 9 mini quizzes. Each quiz will be online, and will be multiple choice, true/false and the like.

There will be a practice mini quiz in Seminar 1.

For Seminars 2 to 9, there will be 5 questions. For seminar 10, there will be 10 questions (which will cover the whole course).

In total, there will be 50 questions. Each question is of equal weight.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Instructions will be provided in class. You will need a computer connected to the Internet in class to do the assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The assessment will be released on or before 4 August 2026.

You will examine the application of privacy law in relation to a current issue. You will have a choice of topics. Further details will be provided on Blackboard. The word limit will be 2,200 words. The word limit should be detailed on the first page of the assessment. Footnotes are not included in the word limit and a bibliography is not required. Any words over the word limit will not be marked.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Step 1: Group presentation 30% (10 mins plus 5 mins Q&A)

By Week Four, you will form a small group (approximately 4 students). You will be given opportunities for group formation during seminars, and you may choose your own members. 

Your final assessment task will be a group presentation.

Topic choice

Your group will choose a topic from a list of provided topics. During the semester we will discuss current privacy laws, but also note areas where the law is inadequate, or is failing to address privacy harms. We will also discuss current and emerging areas of law reform and litigation in respect of pricacy. This task gives you the opportunity to choose an area of law, conduct research, and present your ideas as to how the law could achieve better privacy outcomes.

Due date

In class, you will be shown how to book a session for your group presentation. Session times will be released for week 13. There may also be some times released for the first week of exam block if required (exact date and time TBC, depending on student enrolment numbers).

Format

Each oral presentation will be 15 minutes in total (10 minutes plus 5 minutes for questions). More than one group will present in a session. All group members must be present for the session. Your group will be presenting to the course coordinator and a small group of fellow students.

Handout

Each group must provide a one-page handout to accompany your presentation. The handout should be on one side of A4 paper, and must be printed and provided at the beginning of your presentation. The handout can be text, a slide, a diagram, an info graphic, a photograph, or a combination, provided that the content fits on one page. Powerpoint presentations are not permitted.

Group input

All group members must contribute to selecting, developing, researching, and presenting the topic. The oral component of the presentation may be given by a single group member or some/all of the members (ideally, all group members will present). All group members must be prepared to answer questions during the Q&A component. Every group member will receive the same mark, unless a case is made otherwise. Two assessors will review the presentation (in-person or a recording) to provide moderation of grades.

Questions

Questions can be asked during the presentation.

Each group member will be expected to be able answer any question asked, even if the question relates to subject matter presented by another team member.  That is: Questions may be directed to any member of the group, and all members should be ready to answer questions on any issue.

Resources to support your group work will be provided on the course BlackBoard and discussed in class.

Step 2: Individual reflection 10% (600 words maximum)

Three days after presentation, you will submit an INDIVIDUAL written reflection on your experiences during this task. This will include reflection on your topic choice, your contribution to the work, and the group work process, and privacy law in general. Further guidance will be provided in class. Your reflection paper be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The presentation will be in person. The reflection must be submitted electronically in a Word document via the link on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 1 - What is Privacy? What is Privacy Law?

The conceptual and legal definitional issues. Why do we need privacy? Information Privacy Law: The dominant paradigm of privacy law as control of personal information.


Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Seminar 1 - What is Privacy? What is Privacy Law?
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 1 - What is Privacy?  What is Privacy Law? The conceptual and legal definitional issues. Why do we need privacy? Information Privacy Law: The dominant paradigm of privacy law as control of personal information.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 2 - The Constitutional Balance

The complex issue of balancing privacy with other rights-based considerations privacy and human rights; privacy and free speech; privacy and commercial rights; privacy and our self-determination and bodily-autonomy. This seminar will also consider privacy and the U.S. Bill of Rights 14th amendment.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 2 - The Constitutional Balance The complex issue of balancing privacy with other rights-based considerations   privacy and human rights; privacy and free speech; privacy and commercial rights; privacy and our self-determination and bodily-autonomy. This seminar will also consider privacy and the U.S. Bill of Rights 14th amendment.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 3 - Privacy Torts

Development of privacy torts in Australia and other common law jurisdictions. We will also consider how to value privacy, from a legal viewpoint.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 3 - Privacy Torts Development of privacy torts in Australia and other common law jurisdictions. We will also consider how to value privacy, from a legal viewpoint.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 4 - Introduction to Australian Privacy Law

Introduction to the legislative framework in Australia.

Privacy Act: Consideration APPs:
Examination of privacy principles regarding transparency requirements. Critique of privacy policies and notice and consent.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 4 - Introduction to Australian Privacy Law Introduction to the legislative framework in Australia. Privacy Act: Consideration APPs: Examination of privacy principles regarding transparency requirements. Critique of privacy policies and notice and consent.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 5 - Privacy Act: Collection APPs

Examination of privacy principles that govern the collection of personal information.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 5 - Privacy Act: Collection APPs Examination of privacy principles that govern the collection of personal information.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 6 - Privacy Act: Dealing APPs (Use and Disclosure); Quality, Access and Correction

Examination of privacy principles that govern dealings with personal information including de-identification of personal information.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_title: Seminar 6 - Privacy Act: Dealing APPs (Use and Disclosure); Quality, Access and Correction
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 6 - Privacy Act: Dealing APPs (Use and Disclosure); Quality, Access and Correction Examination of privacy principles that govern dealings with personal information including de-identification of personal information.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 7 - Privacy Act: Security and Data Breaches; Privacy Class Actions

Examination of privacy principles that govern security of and access to personal information. We will consider cybersecurity and the privacy implications of recent ransomware attacks, including the collection and storage of too much information for too long. This seminar will consider other privacy rights, such as the right of correction.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_title: Seminar 7 - Privacy Act: Security and Data Breaches; Privacy Class Actions
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 7 - Privacy Act: Security and Data Breaches; Privacy Class Actions Examination of privacy principles that govern security of and access to personal information. We will consider cybersecurity and the privacy implications of recent ransomware attacks, including the collection and storage of too much information for too long. This seminar will consider other privacy rights, such as the right of correction.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 8 - AI and Privacy Law, and other selected topical areas

This seminar will consider issues arising out of the development and use of AI and its impact on privacy. We will also cover selected topics in privacy law. Students will be asked as to vote on which topics this seminar should consider. Topics that may be discussed in this seminar include:

  • Government Surveillance
  • Facial Recognition
  • Privacy and the media
  • Online privacy
  • Social Media
  • Privacy and health
  • DNA testing and privacy

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Seminar 8 - AI and Privacy Law, and other selected topical areas
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 8 - AI and Privacy Law, and other selected topical areas This seminar will consider issues arising out of the development and use of AI and its impact on privacy.  We will also cover selected topics in privacy law. Students will be asked as to vote on which topics this seminar should consider. Topics that may be discussed in this seminar include: Government Surveillance Facial Recognition Privacy and the media Online privacy Social Media Privacy and health DNA testing and privacy

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 9 - International issues and developments in Privacy Law

The European concept of privacy.

Overview of the GDPR and what it means for Australian businesses.

Examination of the right to be forgotten.

Privacy in China.

Recent privacy developments in the United States.

Generally, what can we learn from privacy laws in other countries and what is the best way to reform Australian privacy law.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Seminar 9 - International issues and developments in Privacy Law
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 9 - International issues and developments in Privacy Law The European concept of privacy. Overview of the GDPR and what it means for Australian businesses. Examination of the right to be forgotten. Privacy in China. Recent privacy developments in the United States. Generally, what can we learn from privacy laws in other countries and what is the best way to reform Australian privacy law.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminar 10 - The Future of Privacy Law?

Consideration of the future direction of privacy law in a Big Data world. Recent Australian law reform involving privacy, current proposals for reform and likely changes.

To wrap up, we will review why we have privacy laws and are the laws fit for the future.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Seminar 10 - The Future of Privacy Law? Consideration of the future direction of privacy law in a Big Data world. Recent Australian law reform involving privacy, current proposals for reform and likely changes. To wrap up, we will review why we have privacy laws and are the laws fit for the future.
